metadata.title: Deciphering the Decline: A Computational Analysis of Two Decades of Canadian Newspaper Op-Eds on Freedom of Information

abstract:
Background: Newspaper op-eds are an underexplored mode of communication that frame social, cultural, and political issues.

Analysis: This article uses an unsupervised machine-learning approach called structural topic modelling to map changes in the content of a corpus of Canadian newspaper opeds on freedom of information (FOI) law spanning a 20-year period. This makes it possible to investigate changes in the content of newspaper op-eds over time and to decipher trends in the kinds of topics that national, regional, and local newspapers publish.

Computational approaches to analyzing news texts are used, and recommendations are offered for future research on FOI and political communications.

paragraphs: Freedom of information (FOI) laws are expanding across the globe, ostensibly making government more transparent (Ackerman & Sandoval-Ballesteros, 2006). Although an increasing number of academics are submitting FOI requests to collect data for their research (Luscombe, Walby, & Lippert, 2017;Walby & Luscombe, 2017, 2018), obtaining meaningful results using FOI is not easy. FOI co-ordinators use informal techniques to limit the amount of information disclosed in any given request (A. Roberts, 2006), including exorbitant fee estimates (Wagner, 2017). One reason for these access barriers is that compliance with FOI laws in many countries tends to be low, which is extensively documented in the literature (Darch & Underwood, 2005). A much lesser studied dimension of FOI law is public opinion. Little is known about how public views of FOI change over time and why. Although literature has shown how compliance fluctuates over time and from one government to the next (Koch & Gottrup, 2019;Lagunes & Pocasangre, 2019;Michener, 2015;Wasike, 2016), less is known about how public opinions regarding FOI evolve over time. Difficulties in filling this knowledge gap are compounded by the increasing challenges of measuring public opinion via traditional means, such as phone-based surveys. This article explores computational analyses of newspaper op-eds as an alternative source of knowledge about public views or opinions regarding FOI.

Analyzing trends in a large sample of op-eds on issues of FOI in Canada, this study explores how structural topic modelling (STM), an unsupervised machinelearning approach, can be used to study newspaper op-eds as a form of political communication. This is intended to be a methodological intervention as much as a substantive empirical one. The approach used in this article is not limited to the study of FOI and could be applied more widely to other forms of journalistic knowledge production, such as social media. Topic modelling is a generative statistical technique for discovering latent "topics" that run through a corpus of documents (Blei, 2012;DiMaggio, Nag, & Blei, 2013;Grimmer & Stewart, 2013;Terman, 2017). Topics are generated using probabilistic models computed from observed word distributions. As mixed-methods researchers, it is important to address the larger question of how computational methods like STM could be further used to support more traditional methods, including content and discourse analysis (also see Luscombe, Duncan, & Walby, 2022;McKelvey, 2014). An equally important topic is how computational approaches can support the temporal analysis of texts, something that can be difficult using traditional techniques alone.

Conceptually, newspaper op-eds are conceived of as a form of mediated, citizen-based political communication with the potential to yield valuable insight into issues of governance and public opinion. In their most basic form, newspaper op-eds are a mode of written political communication that convey public views about issues (Ansary & Babaii, 2005, 2009;Van Dijk, 1995). Editorials are opinions, analyses, and political endorsements written by newspaper editorial boards and staff writers, whereas members of the public typically author op-eds. Editorials may influence elites, while op-eds may have more resonance with everyday citizens (Van Dijk, 1995), which is what makes op-eds a potentially valuable data set for studying public opinion. This study draws from and contributes to existing literature that examines op-eds as a unique form and genre of communication (Ansary & Babaii, 2005, 2009;Bonyadi, 2010;Farrokhi & Nazemi, 2015). One key feature of op-eds is that they tend to conform to a standard format in most countries, a pattern that tends to be robust across newspapers, opening the potential for global comparative research into op-eds as a mode of communication. This standardized, schematic structure (lead, thesis, argument, three points, further proof, return to lead) (Bonyadi, 2010) makes the op-ed especially amenable to analysis using natural language processing techniques, such as STM.

Previous scholarly analysis of journalistic news production has relied primarily on human-driven techniques, such as content and discourse analysis. With the unfolding "computational turn" in the social sciences (Lazer, Pentland, Watts, Aral, Athey, Contractor, Freelon, Gonzalez-Bailon, King, Margetts, Nelson, Salganik, Strohmaier, Vespignani, & Wagner, 2020), new innovative techniques are becoming widely available. A major advantage of these computational techniques is they can reveal patterns that traditional human-driven modes of analysis typically cannot, both in terms of the nature of the insight and the scale at which the analysis takes place (Gabrielatos & Baker, 2008;Nelson, Burk, Knudsen, & McCall, 2021;Olson, Calderon-Figueroa, Bidian, Silver, & Sanner, 2020). Computational methods including lexical network analysis (Rule, Cointet, & Bearman, 2015), term frequency-inverse document frequency analysis (Aizawa, 2003), topic modelling (DiMaggio et al., 2013;Terman, 2017), sentiment analysis (Prabowo & Thelwall, 2009;Taboada, Brooke, Tofiloski, Voll, & Stede, 2011), and word embeddings (Olson et al., 2020;Stoltz & Taylor, 2019), among others, can process and analyze massive amounts of text in a relatively small amount of time. Computational approaches should not be seen as supplanting traditional methods but should instead be considered for the added-value they can bring to existing, especially more human-driven, techniques (Olson et al., 2020).

A large corpus of op-eds on FOI law in Canada spanning a 20-year period (N = 1,707) was used to conduct this analysis. The analysis was refined further by examining how the topic content of op-eds in the corpus changes over time and across newspaper markets. The article concludes by assessing the value of this approach to analyzing news texts and offering several recommendations for future research into FOI and political communication. Computational techniques such as STM show promise as an additional methodological tool for studying public views and political communications, and can be usefully applied before, alongside, and after more traditional qualitative and quantitative methods in a mixedmethods design.

sections.header_raw: FOI, public opinion, and newspaper op-eds
sections.header_norm: foi public opinion and newspaper op eds
paragraphs: FOI requests allow researchers to collect government records that would otherwise never be released (Clifton-Sprigg, James, & Vujic, 2020;A. Roberts, 2006). The problem is that FOI regimes in some jurisdictions can be dismal at releasing records as per the letter of the law (Birkinshaw, 2010;A. Roberts, 2006;Worthy, 2017) as there are many countervailing forces within governments that undermine the administration of FOI. FOI co-ordinators play a key role in the process, although the complexity and difficulties of their work are little-known to people who are not regular FOI users or work in government (Johnson, 2020). Using FOI in the "algorithmic age" can be difficult since some online mechanisms of governance, such as privatized data management solutions, can operate outside of the jurisdiction of FOI (Fink, 2018). FOI processes themselves are increasingly mediated by private software applications that shape the day-to-day work of FOI coordinators and influence outcomes (Carter & Stratton, 2021). Beyond FOI, other avenues for transparency exist, such as open government (Berliner, Ingrams, & Piotrowski, 2018), however, research suggests that open government initiatives are still more rhetoric than reality (Wang & Shepherd, 2020).

The level of transparency that FOI provides in a jurisdiction may shift over time due to several factors (Lagunes & Pocasangre, 2019;Michener, 2015). Local governments, such as those in small cities, may have few funds available for staffing FOI offices or may have less ability to engage in FOI training. These and other factors result in variable levels of compliance even within a single country (Shepherd, Stevenson, & Flinn, 2010). It can be difficult for high levels of compliance to be achieved with FOI laws (Cuillier, 2010;Worthy, John, & Vannoni, 2017), which can have an influence on how effective members of the public feel FOI is at enhancing government openness. Countries with new FOI laws can experience the highest levels of citizen approval of FOI following implementation, although this support can decline over time if restrictions on information access and press freedom are introduced (Camaj, 2016;Darch & Underwood, 2005). The idea that FOI laws can enhance government transparency as well as general levels of public support for FOI needs to be tested in multiple jurisdictions and over time (Grimmelikhuijsen, John, Meijer, & Worthy, 2018).

Public use of and support for FOI can fluctuate depending on numerous factors, such as rates of compliance and public awareness. Using a survey methodology, David Cuillier and Bruce Pinkleton (2011) examined the relationship between public views of FOI and political ideology, generating results about which demographic and ideological factors predict support for FOI and other government transparency initiatives. They found skepticism, cynicism, apathy, and liberalism are predictive of support for government openness and transparency. More research is needed to assess public views of FOI, particularly research that uses novel methods that can supplement techniques such as traditional surveys. It is often not feasible to conduct large-scale surveys given the time and resources involved.

One possible proxy for public opinion worth exploring is the newspaper oped. Op-eds are a genre of political communication in newspapers that can reflect diverse political positions despite the uniformity of form imposed by newspapers (Ansary & Babaii, 2005). Even in a cross-cultural context, the generic form of opeds allows them to be compared to assess the variation in terms of politics and rhetoric (Al-Khatib & Alqaoud, 2021;Ansary & Babaii, 2009). Op-eds are unique as a form of newspaper communication because they take an explicit political, social, or cultural position and attempt to persuade the reader of the veracity of their position. Opinion pieces can also reflect master narratives or tropes (Hackett & Zhao, 1994) that convey big ideas in a small number of words (i.e., "crooked elites"). In this way, op-eds should not be treated as sources of original knowledge but rather as mirrors of broad trends in politics and opinion. Unlike hard news reporting that prides itself on values of neutrality and balance (but see Lehman-Wilzig & Seletzky, 2010), op-eds are polemical and, in this way, serve as superior potential proxies for public opinion (Farrokhi & Nazemi, 2015). Surveys will always provide more fine-grained data and allow researchers to control facets of the data set more effectively. Nonetheless, op-eds do represent public opinion at some level and are, therefore, deserving of analysis, especially given their ease of accessibility and amenability to computational techniques.

Opinion and editorial contributions in newspapers can have long-lasting effects on public opinion and are deserving of study as a proxy for public views (Atanasova & Koteyko, 2017;Coppock, Ekins, & Kirby, 2018). The difference between large or elite newspaper opinion or editorial contributions (Izadi & Saghaye-Biria, 2007) and local and regional newspaper op-eds is that the latter represent more lay public opinions rather than professional, academic, or paid pundit viewpoints (Rasinger, 2010). Newspapers are known to adopt different political positions based on ownership (Gilens & Hertzman, 2000), which influences the types of op-eds that any newspaper is likely to publish. Some newspapers are more likely to publish more socially conservative editorials and opinion pieces (Izadi & Saghaye-Biria, 2007), determined in large part by the political economy of newspaper ownership as well as the purview of editorial staff. Building on these insights as well as broader trends in computational social science (see Luscombe, Duncan, & Walby, 2022;Luscombe, Dick, & Walby, 2022), this article explores how topics related to FOI in Canadian newspaper op-eds have changed over time and across national, regional, and local markets. By doing so, it contributes to emerging debates about computational methods in communication and journalism studies (Boumans & Trilling, 2016;Coddington, 2015;Lewis, Zamith, & Hermida, 2013;Zamith & Lewis, 2015).

Countries with new FOI laws can experience the highest levels of citizen approval of FOI following implementation, although this support can decline over time if restrictions on information access and press freedom are introduced (Camaj, 2016;Darch & Underwood, 2005). The idea that FOI laws can enhance government transparency as well as general levels of public support for FOI needs to be tested in multiple jurisdictions and over time (Grimmelikhuijsen, John, Meijer, & Worthy, 2018).

Public use of and support for FOI can fluctuate depending on numerous factors, such as rates of compliance and public awareness. Using a survey methodology, David Cuillier and Bruce Pinkleton (2011) examined the relationship between public views of FOI and political ideology, generating results about which demographic and ideological factors predict support for FOI and other government transparency initiatives. They found skepticism, cynicism, apathy, and liberalism are predictive of support for government openness and transparency. More research is needed to assess public views of FOI, particularly research that uses novel methods that can supplement techniques such as traditional surveys. It is often not feasible to conduct large-scale surveys given the time and resources involved.

One possible proxy for public opinion worth exploring is the newspaper oped. Op-eds are a genre of political communication in newspapers that can reflect diverse political positions despite the uniformity of form imposed by newspapers (Ansary & Babaii, 2005). Even in a cross-cultural context, the generic form of opeds allows them to be compared to assess the variation in terms of politics and rhetoric (Al-Khatib & Alqaoud, 2021;Ansary & Babaii, 2009). Op-eds are unique as a form of newspaper communication because they take an explicit political, social, or cultural position and attempt to persuade the reader of the veracity of their position. Opinion pieces can also reflect master narratives or tropes (Hackett & Zhao, 1994) that convey big ideas in a small number of words (i.e., "crooked elites"). In this way, op-eds should not be treated as sources of original knowledge but rather as mirrors of broad trends in politics and opinion. Unlike hard news reporting that prides itself on values of neutrality and balance (but see Lehman-Wilzig & Seletzky, 2010), op-eds are polemical and, in this way, serve as superior potential proxies for public opinion (Farrokhi & Nazemi, 2015). Surveys will always provide more fine-grained data and allow researchers to control facets of the data set more effectively. Nonetheless, op-eds do represent public opinion at some level and are, therefore, deserving of analysis, especially given their ease of accessibility and amenability to computational techniques.

Opinion and editorial contributions in newspapers can have long-lasting effects on public opinion and are deserving of study as a proxy for public views (Atanasova & Koteyko, 2017;Coppock, Ekins, & Kirby, 2018). The difference between large or elite newspaper opinion or editorial contributions (Izadi & Saghaye-Biria, 2007) and local and regional newspaper op-eds is that the latter represent more lay public opinions rather than professional, academic, or paid pundit viewpoints (Rasinger, 2010). Newspapers are known to adopt different political positions based on ownership (Gilens & Hertzman, 2000), which influences the types of op-eds that any newspaper is likely to publish. Some newspapers are more likely to publish more socially conservative editorials and opinion pieces (Izadi & Saghaye-Biria, 2007), determined in large part by the political economy of newspaper ownership as well as the purview of editorial staff. Building on these insights as well as broader trends in computational social science (see Luscombe, Duncan, & Walby, 2022;Luscombe, Dick, & Walby, 2022), this article explores how topics related to FOI in Canadian newspaper op-eds have changed over time and across national, regional, and local markets. By doing so, it contributes to emerging debates about computational methods in communication and journalism studies (Boumans & Trilling, 2016;Coddington, 2015;Lewis, Zamith, & Hermida, 2013;Zamith & Lewis, 2015).

sections.paper_id: Luscombe and Walby - 2022 - Deciphering the Decline A Computational Analysis of Two Decades of Canadian Newspaper Op-Eds on Fre
paragraphs: The purpose of this study is: 1) to examine what a popular unsupervised machine learning approach-structural topic modelling-can reveal about shifts in news media representations over time and across newspaper markets; and 2) to explore how the empirical patterns discovered by these techniques can be used to study op-eds as a mode of political communication and supplement more traditional techniques popular in communication studies. This inquiry is valuable for the possibilities it opens in the realm of future qualitative and mixed-methods research. The analysis uses STM's regression modelling capabilities to investigate documentlevel trends in topic content over time and across newspaper markets and construct a topic correlation network of document-level co-occurrence. 1 This approach can be usefully applied to detect both macro trends in topic content and withinsample variations and anomalies that invite further analysis and interpretation in future research (Brant & Timmermans, 2021). Given the potential for computational approaches to reveal trends in news production (De Grove, Boghe, & De Marez, 2020;Segal & Soffer, 2020;Vallance-Jones, 2013), these techniques will likely be of interest to those working in the field of journalism studies.

To construct the corpus, English language op-eds on FOI were collected from ProQuest's Canadian Newsstream database. The search parameters only included articles from 1999-2019 that were labelled as op-eds, excluding duplicates. The search terms were "freedom of information" and "access to information." 2 To ensure completeness, several additional manual searches were conducted, particularly where there was reason to believe that newspapers op-eds had not been correctly tagged in the database. The final data set consisted of 1,879 newspaper op-eds, which was further paired down to 1,752 during preprocessing, due to duplicates and unrelated articles. The decision was made to exclude op-eds published in local news sources in the Maritimes and Canadian territories, as the number of op-eds from these regions was low (n < 5) and potentially incomplete. Using a set of custom string detection algorithms written in the statistical programming language R, the full text and corresponding metadata-publisher, date, provincewas extracted from each article. 3 The publisher and date metadata for each op-ed was used to construct the two covariate measures used in the STM analysis: year and newspaper market.

The op-eds in the final corpus were not evenly distributed over time, showing a gradual decline for op-eds on FOI in Canada beginning in 2010. The frequency of total op-eds in Canada did not follow the same overall pattern of decline (see Figure 1). The year 2010 was also when the greatest number of op-eds on FOI in Canada were published in the 20-year timeframe. This is not surprising, given 2010 was an especially tumultuous year for the Canadian federal government, with then-Prime Minister Stephen Harper proroguing the House of Commons and the Afghan detainee inquiry raising questions about secrecy and transparency (CBC News, 2010). The 10-year decline in the number of op-eds on FOI starting in 2010 also appears to inversely mirror the increase in social media use and alternative news media sites in Canada (EMarketer, 2019). Most of the op-eds in the corpus came from newspapers based in Ontario, which is accounted for by the fact that it is Canada's largest province and the home of some of Canada's largest, most influential newspapers, including its three national outlets, the Globe & Mail, Toronto Star, and National Post. Op-eds published by newspapers based in Québec were notably small, however, this is at least partly explained by the fact only English 0 20,000 40,000 60,000 0 25 50 75 100 125 Ontario British Columbia Manitoba Alberta Quebec Saskatchewan 1999 2009 2019 1999 2009 2019 1999 2009 2019 1999 2009 2019 1999 2009 2019 1999 2009 2019 0 20 40 60 80 Number of op eds B Local National Regional 1999 2009 2019 1999 2009 2019 1999 2009 2019 0 25 50 75 100 C Notes: Total number of FOI-related op-eds in Canada over time, excluding New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, and Yukon Territory. Panel A shows the total volume of op-eds on FOI over time and all op-eds over time. Panel B shows the total volume of op-eds on FOI over time by province. Panel C shows the total volume of op-eds on FOI over time by newspaper market.

language op-eds have been collected. In terms of market, most op-eds in the corpus came from what were coded as local newspapers. Finally, although the general decline appears to have held true after disaggregating by province and market, there were two exceptions. Op-eds on FOI in Manitoba showed a slight increase over time, as did op-eds on FOI published in regional level newspapers. The issue of declining op-ed frequency is revisited in the conclusion.

The package "stm" in the programming language R was used to conduct the STM analysis (Roberts, Stewart, & Tingley, 2014). 4 The main difference between STM and other popular topic modelling algorithms (e.g., latent Dirichlet allocation) is that STM allows the researcher to include metadata in their analysis as model priors (Lucas, Nielsen, Roberts, Stewart, Storer, & Tingley, 2015). The output of STM makes it easier for scholars to use regression modelling in measuring the extent to which changes in the prevalence of topic distributions are predicted by changes in document-level metadata, in this case year and newspaper market. To prepare the documents for analysis, standard text preprocessing procedures were observed: converting all text to lower case and removing English stop words (a, the, but, etc.), numbers, punctuation, and unwanted symbols (e.g., $, &) from the full text of each op-ed. As is standard before estimating topic models, individual words in each article were reduced to their root using the Porter Snowball II stemming algorithm (e.g., accessing was reduced to access). The final corpus consisted of 1,707 documents and 18,126 unique words after preprocessing.

A common challenge with topic modelling is that there is no single "right" answer regarding the number of topics one should fit a given corpus to (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). The current standard is to run multiple models, each with a different number of topics K, and compare the results. The general rule of thumb in topic modelling is to minimize rather than maximize the number of topics, as larger numbers of topics come at the expense of human interpretability. For this analysis, 10 different models were estimated to begin, with K-scores of 5 through 50 in increments of five, selecting the 20-topic STM model as the best fit. Subsequently, the outputs of nine additional models with K-scores 16 through 24 in increments of one were compared. Comparing these additional models confirmed that the 20-topic model was indeed the best fit for this study.

Topic model selection or "hyperparameter optimization" is not easy. Deliberation on model selection occurred for several days. Each model was assessed using the data-driven strategies documented in Margaret Roberts, Brandon Stewart, and Dustin Tingley (2014), paired with expert interpretation based on years of researching FOI law and policy in Canada (Luscombe et al., 2017;Walby & Luscombe, 2018, 2021). 5 In terms of data-driven strategies, residual checks were conducted and the held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, and exclusivity scores for each model were examined (Roberts et al., 2014). These metrics are useful but imperfect and are best used together and in combination with human judgement. Residual checks involve testing for variance overdispersion in STM's data generating process (Taddy, 2012). Held-out likelihood captures how well a model performs when presented with data it has not seen before. Semantic coherence measures the degree of cooccurrence among top-weighted words in a topic (Mimno, Wallach, Talley, Leenders, & McCallum, 2011). Exclusivity estimates how exclusive top-weighted words are to a particular topic (Airoldi & Bischof, 2016). Qualitatively, each model was assessed by analyzing lists of the top-weighted words for each topic, sometimes also generating samples of top-weighted passages for deeper analysis. Together, these strategies made it possible to identify the model that was the most meaningful and interpretable from a human (rather than machine) perspective.

Once the 20-topic model was settled on, FOI issues and news reporting in Canada were drawn on to ascribe labels to each of the topics (which are otherwise just ranked vectors of words associated with the topic). The topics were constructed by examining a combination of the top-weighted and most exclusive words and sample passages generated for each topic based on probability distributions (see Figure 2). 6 To learn more about the model selection process, including the mathematical basis for the above metrics and how to interpret them, see the Supplementary Materials in Luscombe (2022).

Luscombe and Walby - 2022 - Deciphering the Decline A Computational Analysis of Two Decades of Canadian Newspaper Op-Eds on Fre
paragraphs: The STM model reveals a wide range of topics, from issues related to FOI compliance and oversight to public policing and accountability and from Indigenous pol- Fiscal Policy Federal Politics & Liberal Scandals Federal Politics & the Harper Conservatives Information, Law & Governance Health Care & Information Governance Municipal Politics Journalism & News Production Environment, Agriculture & Development Online Data & Internet Technology Diversity, Education, & Citizenship Provincial Information Commisioners & Compliance Military & Defence Gun Control & Database Politics Foreign Affairs & Trade Public Police & Accountability Security & Corrections Intergovernmental Relations Law & Justice Procedures Indigenous Politics Topic 20 Topic 14 Topic 16 Topic 18 Topic 6 Topic 9 Topic 12 Topic 17 Topic 11 Topic 5 Topic 2 Topic 3 Topic 15 Topic 8 Topic 7 Topic 1 Topic 4 Topic 10 Topic 19 Topic 13 0.0% 5.0% 10.0% 15.0% 20.0%

itics to foreign affairs. The most prevalent topic in the corpus was labelled "Federal Information Commissioners & Compliance." Through analyzing the most probable and exclusive words related to this topic, as well as generating sample passages from the larger corpus, this topic was determined to be specifically related to FOI oversight and compliance at the federal level. On its face, this finding makes sense. Although every province and territory in Canada has its own FOI law, Canada's federal access law, the Access to Information Act (Walby & Luscombe, 2018), tends to receive much more public political attention. Based on an analysis of top-weighted passages, op-eds with content most associated with this topic discussed matters such as federal government openness, accountability, and public trust, with many op-eds expressing particular concern with the ability of the federal information commissioner to uphold these "fundamental pillars" of democracy, as one op-ed put it. The discrepancy in prevalence between this first theme and its provincial-level counterpart, "Provincial Information Commissioners & Compliance," further corroborates the well-known trend in Canadian FOI wherein the federal system receives more public, political, and media attention than provincial ones.

Op-eds related to the most prevalent topic in the corpus, "Federal Information Commissioners & Compliance," are used as a vehicle to advance critiques of the broken access system in Canada, which several federal information commissioners have commented on in their annual reports in the last two decades (Canada, 2002). The dominance of federal law and politics is further reflected in the third and fourth most prevalent topics in the corpus, "Federal Politics & Liberal Scandals" and "Federal Politics & the Harper Conservatives." paragraphs: A regression analysis of topic prevalence on year reveals additional macro-level trends in public discourse related to FOI over time (see Figure 3). Linear modelling shows significant negative relationships in four topics: "Information, Law & Governance" (p < 0.001), "Federal Politics & Liberal Scandals" (p < 0.001), "Federal Information Commissioners & Compliance" (p < 0.05), and "Fiscal Policy" (p < 0.01). There are significant positive relationships in six of the topics in the model: "Federal Politics & the Harper Conservatives" (p < 0.001), "Municipal Politics" (p < 0.05), "Journalism & News Production" (p < 0.001), "Diversity, Education & Citizenship" (p < 0.05), "Law & Justice Procedures" (p < 0.05), and "Intergovernmental Relations" (p < 0.001). The topic label "Intergovernmental Relations" refers to opinion pieces referencing more than one order or level of government. The remaining topics yielded no statistically significant positive or negative relationships with year.

Finally, some topics appear to be more associated with certain news markets, which were measured in three levels-national, regional, and local-with local being the referent category in the models (see Figure 4). National newspapers were more likely than local to be associated with the topics of "Gun Control & Database Politics" (p < 0.01), "Federal Information Commissioners & Compliance" (p < 0.05), "Military & Defence" (p < 0.001), and "Security & Corrections" (p < 0.01). To no surprise, both national (p < 0.001) and regional (p < 0.05) were less likely to be associated with op-eds concerning the topic of "Municipal Politics." National newspapers were less likely than local to be associated with op-eds on issues of "Intergovernmental Relations" (p < 0.01), while regional newspapers were more likely than local newspapers to be associated with op-eds on this topic (p < 0.05). Finally, regional newspapers were less likely than local to be associated with op-eds related to the topic of "Journalism & News Production" (p < 0.05). The remaining relationships between topics and our three levels of market were not statistically significant. Notes: Newspaper market and expected topic prevalence (regression coefficient) for each topic in the estimated STM model. Lines indicate 95 percent confidence intervals for each point estimate.

paragraphs: . This analysis provides a means of visualizing both the prevalence of each topic in the corpus (similar to Figure 2) as well as the strength of correlation between each of the topics, where a positive correlation is represented as the edge, the labelled topic from the STM model is represented as the node, and the overall prevalence of each topic in the corpus is represented as the node size. Larger sized edges between nodes indicate stronger positive correlations. When the strength of correlation is below the set threshold (here, a very low threshold of r > = 0.01), no edge is drawn between nodes. Topic correlation in this case equates to topic co-occurrence-the more positively correlated two or more topics are, the more likely they are to co-appear within the same document or op-ed. Topic correlations can also help strengthen confidence in the choice of estimated model and in the labels applied to each topic. Notes: Topic correlation network. Node size indicates topic prevalence in the corpus. sections.paper_id: Luscombe and Walby - 2022 - Deciphering the Decline A Computational Analysis of Two Decades of Canadian Newspaper Op-Eds on Fre
paragraphs: Computational methods are powerful tools for making sense of large amounts of communications in a relatively short amount of time and with relatively few resources (Grimmer & Stewart, 2013). Computational approaches to text analysis such as the one used in this article can identify trends in ways that more micro-level qualitative studies cannot. As Paul Baker, Costas Gabrielatos, Majid Khosravinik, Michal Krzyzanowski, Tony McEnery, and Ruth Wodak (2008) argue, combining a corpusbased approach with more traditional qualitative approaches such as discourse analyses can shed additional insight on trends and patterns in texts such as news communications.

The goal of this study was to assess evidence for what appears to be a significant decline in FOI op-eds in Canada while remaining open to emergent patterns and surprises in the data. Below is a reflection on the broader meaning and implications of the findings, highlighting the possibilities they open for traditional qualitative techniques. Using structural topic modelling, 20 overarching themes pertaining to FOI were identified in the corpus. These ranged from issues of oversight to law and policing and municipal politics. Mapping the substantive content of op-eds on FOI in this way revealed that federal topics related to FOI were more prominent than regional/local themes, such as municipal politics. By quantifying the relationship of each document in the corpus to each of the 20 topics, it was possible to use regression modelling to identify more macro-level trends over time and across newspaper markets. It was also possible to examine topic co-occurrence in each of the op-eds in the corpus by adopting a network approach.

Surprisingly, topics such as "Gun Control & Database Politics" emerge with a reasonably high degree of prevalence across the corpus. Although less surprising, it is also noteworthy that the least prevalent topic identified in the corpus was "Indigenous Politics," a pattern that echoes the longstanding claims of Indigenous leaders and scholars that the concerns of Indigenous communities about the injustices they have faced due to colonialism and ongoing dispossession are ignored by most Canadians. It is significant that a general decline for both "Federal Information Commissioners & Compliance" as well as "Provincial Information Commissioners & Compliance" begins in 2015, indicating that people may be growing less interested in or hopeful about the work that these institutions promise to do for Canadian access issues. This analysis of the newspaper market helps shed additional light on aspects of this decline in FOI-related op-eds while revealing valuable insights into the kinds of topics national, regional, and local newspapers prefer to publish FOI-related op-eds on. Topics further appeared to be largely event oriented, many of them increasing in topic prevalence around the time of key social and political events such as the events of September 11, 2001, the infamous Liberal sponsorship scandal, and federal elections.

There are limits to what computational methods such as STM can reveal about the meaning and social behind the corpus, but these limits can function as openings for creating samples of data to explore using qualitative inquiry in communication and journalism studies. Rather than being the end of analysis, these findings should be taken as a starting point and complement a more traditional analysis, such as discourse analysis, which involves qualitatively analyzing connotations and patterns of association appearing in texts as well as connections or intertextualities between texts or records, among other elements (Tonkiss, 2004). Digging deeper into op-eds could also entail examining the professional backgrounds and demographic characteristics of the authors. The prevalence of a topic in a specific time span provides clues as to where to look closer to reveal these layers of meaning. As Reiner Grundmann (2021) notes, computational approaches to communications research can be used to prepare samples for further qualitative inquiry or even additional computational comparisons.

There are also political and economic trends that are structural in nature that impact journalism and political communications in Canada (Winseck, 2010;Wilkinson & Winseck, 2019), and although computational methods can provide an outline of these trends, further qualitative and investigative research is needed to explore patterns and eddies within these currents. Computational methods can be used to supplement or orient qualitative studies of communication, but they are not a replacement for finer-grained approaches to analyzing content, discourse, signifiers, and metaphors.

sections.paper_id: Luscombe and Walby - 2022 - Deciphering the Decline A Computational Analysis of Two Decades of Canadian Newspaper Op-Eds on Fre
paragraphs: Contributing to growing discussions of computational methods in communication and journalism studies (De Grove et al., 2020;Segal & Soffer, 2020;Vallance-Jones, 2013), this article applies structural topic modelling to analyze a large corpus of newspaper op-eds on FOI law in Canada. STM was used to examine the relationship between the topic content of the op-eds in the corpus and two covariates: year and newspaper market. This approach made it possible to systematically investigate and visualize changes in the substantive content of newspaper op-eds over time and to decipher trends in the kinds of topics that national, regional, and local newspapers did and did not publish over the past 20 years. Methods such as STM offer a potent means of analyzing news communications and can be used to guide and support additional analyses, particularly those based on more traditional, in-depth qualitative methods such as discourse analysis. Computational methods such as STM allow scholarly analysis to look across a broader span of time and space, both quickly and with relatively few resources. To conclude, the frequency of op-eds on FOI in Canada, which appear to have been steadily declining since 2010, is discussed.

The decline in the number of op-eds on FOI documented in this study may be reflective of a trend toward disengagement, either from the newspaper op-ed, issues of information access and FOI, or both. Newspaper articles including opeds are vital means of framing key social, cultural, and political issues in society, but this may be decreasing with respect to FOI. The decline in the volume of opeds on FOI over time inversely mirrors the rise of social media and alternative media sites in Canada. Online and social media use by Canadians officially surpassed traditional news consumption between 2008-2010(EMarketer, 2019)), the same timespan when op-eds on FOI began to decline in this analysis. FOI and transparency advocacy in Canada may be happening more in social media spaces, whereas two decades ago the newspaper op-ed would have been a more popular medium. However, if it is true that Canadians are increasingly expressing opinions about FOI in "new" digital fora, such as Twitter and Facebook, this is not true for all issues. The total number of op-eds published in Canada does not follow the same declining trend as op-eds on FOI (see Figure 1), suggesting that if people are increasingly retreating to new forms of media to express their ideas and opinions (Vissers & Stolle, 2014), this pattern is unfolding unevenly. This is an important disjuncture worthy of future investigation in communication studies and journalism studies.

Another possible explanation could be disengagement from issues of access and FOI. Rather than taking less interest in the form of the newspaper op-ed, people may be growing less interested in publicly expressing ideas and opinions about FOI altogether. Today, many political institutions are failing to fulfill their intended functions, and even FOI is reaching this point despite the progressive aura that open records or sunshine laws emit when indexed to government transparency (Kazmierski, 2016). Treasury Board of Canada statistics on the administration of Canada's federal FOI regime reveal a system fraught with numerous systemic issues (Kazmierski, 2016;A. Roberts, 2006;Roziere & Walby, 2020), from low-quality service to high rates of abandonment and redaction (Duncan, Luscombe, & Walby, Forthcoming). Although the overall number of requests made under Canada's federal FOI law has remained relatively stable over time (Canada, 2020), it is conceivable that faith in the many fixes and reforms this system needs (and that federal politicians have been promising Canadians for decades) may be declining, which could be the basis for a decline in op-ed engagement with issues of FOI.

A final possibility worth examining pertains to the publishing preferences of newspaper editors. Faced with declines in sales and advertising dollars, newspaper editors may be opting to publish op-eds on issues that they perceive to draw more public attention and readership (in the form of newspaper purchases and clicks online, see Vermeer & Trilling, 2020) than FOI. Rather than a monocausal explanation, there is likely a confluence of factors driving these trends. Increasing flight to online media, mounting pessimism toward Canada's FOI regime, and changing editorial preferences are just three possible factors.

Future research regarding op-eds on FOI and other social issues should also strive to be international and comparative, assessing whether these topics manifest in similar ways in other countries. Such a contribution would further enrich understandings of newspaper communication in both communication and journalism studies. Future research could use traditional qualitative content or discourse analysis as well as other qualitative approaches on select samples to add "depth" and "thickness" to the more general insights of computational analysis (see Jemielniak, 2020). STM analysis can be used before, alongside, and after traditional qualitative techniques in a mixed-method research design. Combining computational methods with traditional approaches in qualitative inquiry is a powerful means of examining political communication and the textual dimensions of social, political, and cultural phenomena.

The total number of op-eds published in Canada does not follow the same declining trend as op-eds on FOI (see Figure 1), suggesting that if people are increasingly retreating to new forms of media to express their ideas and opinions (Vissers & Stolle, 2014), this pattern is unfolding unevenly. This is an important disjuncture worthy of future investigation in communication studies and journalism studies.

Another possible explanation could be disengagement from issues of access and FOI. Rather than taking less interest in the form of the newspaper op-ed, people may be growing less interested in publicly expressing ideas and opinions about FOI altogether. Today, many political institutions are failing to fulfill their intended functions, and even FOI is reaching this point despite the progressive aura that open records or sunshine laws emit when indexed to government transparency (Kazmierski, 2016). Treasury Board of Canada statistics on the administration of Canada's federal FOI regime reveal a system fraught with numerous systemic issues (Kazmierski, 2016;A. Roberts, 2006;Roziere & Walby, 2020), from low-quality service to high rates of abandonment and redaction (Duncan, Luscombe, & Walby, Forthcoming). Although the overall number of requests made under Canada's federal FOI law has remained relatively stable over time (Canada, 2020), it is conceivable that faith in the many fixes and reforms this system needs (and that federal politicians have been promising Canadians for decades) may be declining, which could be the basis for a decline in op-ed engagement with issues of FOI.

A final possibility worth examining pertains to the publishing preferences of newspaper editors. Faced with declines in sales and advertising dollars, newspaper editors may be opting to publish op-eds on issues that they perceive to draw more public attention and readership (in the form of newspaper purchases and clicks online, see Vermeer & Trilling, 2020) than FOI. Rather than a monocausal explanation, there is likely a confluence of factors driving these trends. Increasing flight to online media, mounting pessimism toward Canada's FOI regime, and changing editorial preferences are just three possible factors.

Future research regarding op-eds on FOI and other social issues should also strive to be international and comparative, assessing whether these topics manifest in similar ways in other countries. Such a contribution would further enrich understandings of newspaper communication in both communication and journalism studies. Future research could use traditional qualitative content or discourse analysis as well as other qualitative approaches on select samples to add "depth" and "thickness" to the more general insights of computational analysis (see Jemielniak, 2020). STM analysis can be used before, alongside, and after traditional qualitative techniques in a mixed-method research design. Combining computational methods with traditional approaches in qualitative inquiry is a powerful means of examining political communication and the textual dimensions of social, political, and cultural phenomena.

Contributing to growing discussions of computational methods in communication and journalism studies (De Grove et al., 2020;Segal & Soffer, 2020;Vallance-Jones, 2013), this article applies structural topic modelling to analyze a large corpus of newspaper op-eds on FOI law in Canada. STM was used to examine the relationship between the topic content of the op-eds in the corpus and two covariates: year and newspaper market.
